name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا غَنِمْتُم مِّن شَىْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُۥ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ وَٱلْيَتَـٰمَىٰ وَٱلْمَسَـٰكِينِ وَٱبْنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ إِن كُنتُمْ ءَامَنتُم بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أَنزَلْنَا عَلَىٰ عَبْدِنَا يَوْمَ ٱلْفُرْقَانِ يَوْمَ ٱلْتَقَى ٱلْجَمْعَانِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٤١)
```

```english
Know that whatever thing you may come by, a fifth of it is for Allah and the Apostle, for the relatives and the orphans, for the needy and the traveller, if you have faith in Allah and what We sent down to Our servant on the Day of Separation, the day when the two hosts met; and Allah has power over all things. (41)
```

```arabic
إِذْ أَنتُم بِٱلْعُدْوَةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُم بِٱلْعُدْوَةِ ٱلْقُصْوَىٰ وَٱلرَّكْبُ أَسْفَلَ مِنكُمْ ۚ وَلَوْ تَوَاعَدتُّمْ لَٱخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِى ٱلْمِيعَـٰدِ ۙ وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَقْضِىَ ٱللَّهُ أَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُولًا لِّيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنۢ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيَىٰ مَنْ حَىَّ عَنۢ بَيِّنَةٍ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَسَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٤٢)
```

```english
When you were on the nearer side, and they on the farther side, while the caravan was below you, and had you agreed together on an encounter, you would have certainly failed to keep the tryst, but in order that Allah may carry through a matter that was bound to be fulfilled, so that he who perishes might perish by a manifest proof, and he who lives may live on by a manifest proof, and Allah is indeed all-hearing, all-knowing. (42)
```

```arabic
إِذْ يُرِيكَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ فِى مَنَامِكَ قَلِيلًا ۖ وَلَوْ أَرَىٰكَهُمْ كَثِيرًا لَّفَشِلْتُمْ وَلَتَنَـٰزَعْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَلَّمَ ۗ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٤٣)
```

```english
When Allah showed them to you as few in your dream, and had He shown them as many, you would have lost heart, and disputed about the matter. But Allah spared you. Indeed He knows well what is in the breasts. (43)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ ٱلْتَقَيْتُمْ فِىٓ أَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيلًا وَيُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِىٓ أَعْيُنِهِمْ لِيَقْضِىَ ٱللَّهُ أَمْرًا كَانَ مَفْعُولًا ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٤٤)
```

```english
And when He showed them to you as few in your eyes, when you met them [on the battlefield], and He made you [appear] few in their eyes, [it was] in order that Allah may carry through a matter that was bound to be fulfilled, and to Allah all matters are returned. (44)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا لَقِيتُمْ فِئَةً فَٱثْبُتُوا۟ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٤٥)
```

```english
O you who have faith! When you meet a host [in battle], then stand firm, and remember Allah greatly so that you may be felicitous. (45)
```

```arabic
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَلَا تَنَـٰزَعُوا۟ فَتَفْشَلُوا۟ وَتَذْهَبَ رِيحُكُمْ ۖ وَٱصْبِرُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مَعَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ (٤٦)
```

```english
And obey Allah and His Apostle, and do not dispute, or you will lose heart and your power will be gone. And be patient; indeed Allah is with the patient. (46)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِم بَطَرًا وَرِئَآءَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ (٤٧)
```

```english
Do not be like those who left their homes vainly and to show off to the people, and to bar [other people] from the way of Allah, and Allah comprehends what they do. (47)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ وَقَالَ لَا غَالِبَ لَكُمُ ٱلْيَوْمَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَإِنِّى جَارٌ لَّكُمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَرَآءَتِ ٱلْفِئَتَانِ نَكَصَ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ وَقَالَ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّنكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ مَا لَا تَرَوْنَ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٤٨)
```

```english
When Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them, and said [to the faithless], ‘No one shall overcome you today from among all mankind, and I will stand by you.’ But when the two hosts sighted each other, he took to his heels, saying, ‘Indeed I am quit of you. I see what you do not see. Indeed I fear Allah, and Allah is severe in retribution.’ (48)
```

```arabic
إِذْ يَقُولُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ غَرَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ دِينُهُمْ ۗ وَمَن يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٤٩)
```

```english
When the hypocrites said, and [also] those in whose hearts is a sickness, ‘Their religion has deceived them.’ But whoever puts his trust in Allah, then Allah is indeed all-mighty, all-wise. (49)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ تَرَىٰٓ إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۙ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبَـٰرَهُمْ وَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (٥٠)
```

```english
Were you to see when the angels take away the faithless, striking their faces and their backs, [saying], ‘Taste the punishment of the burning. (50)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (٥١)
```

```english
That is because of what your hands have sent ahead, and because Allah is not tyrannical to the servants.’ (51)
```

```arabic
كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ ۙ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٥٢)
```

```english
Like the precedent of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them, who defied Allah’s signs, so Allah seized them for their sins. Indeed Allah is all-strong, severe in retribution. (52)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّرًا نِّعْمَةً أَنْعَمَهَا عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ حَتَّىٰ يُغَيِّرُوا۟ مَا بِأَنفُسِهِمْ ۙ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٥٣)
```

```english
That is because Allah never changes a blessing that He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls, and Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing: (53)
```

```arabic
كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ ۙ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُم بِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَأَغْرَقْنَآ ءَالَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ وَكُلٌّ كَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٥٤)
```

```english
Like the precedent of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them, who denied the signs of their Lord; so We destroyed them for their sins, and We drowned Pharaoh’s clan; and they were all wrongdoers. (54)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses contain the command to pay the one-fifth (الخمس) of the spoils, the order to remain steadfast upon encountering the enemy and to remember Allah, and a reminder of some of the calamities with which Allah afflicted the enemies of the religion (الدين), disgracing them through divine stratagem (المكر الإلهي). He applied to them the same precedent (سنة) He applied to the folk of Pharaoh and those before them who denied the signs (آيات) of Allah and barred others from His path.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَلِلرَّسُولِ» **"Know that whatever thing you may take as spoils, one-fifth of it is for Allah and the Apostle..."** (8:41) to the end of the verse. **Spoils (الغنم)** and **booty (الغنيمة)** refer to acquiring a benefit through trade, work, or war. Based on the context of the verse's revelation, it applies to the spoils of war. Al-Raghib said: *Al-Ghanam* (الغنم) — with two short 'a' vowels — is well-known; he said: "And of the oxen and the sheep (الغنم), We forbade them their fat." *Al-Ghunm* (الغنم) — with a 'u' followed by a sukun — refers to acquiring and gaining victory over something. It was then used for everything gained from enemies or others. He said: «وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ» ("Know that whatever thing you may take as spoils") and «فَكُلُوا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلالاً طَيِّباً» ("So eat of what you have taken as spoils, such as is lawful and good"). *Al-Maghnam* (المغنم) is that which is taken as spoils, and its plural is *maghanim* (مغانم); He said: "With Allah are abundant spoils (مغانم)." End quote.

"The kinsmen" (ذو القربى) refers to the relative. What is meant here is the kinship of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله or specific individuals among them, as interpreted by definitive narrations (الآثار القطعية). The **orphan (اليتيم)** is the human being whose father died while he was a child. It is said: every animal is an orphan from its mother's side except for the human, whose orphanhood is from the father's side.

Regarding His saying: «فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ» ("then indeed for Allah is its fifth"), it has been recited with the opening (فتح) of the *hamza* in *anna* (أن). It is possible that a preposition is implied, meaning: "Know that whatever you take as spoils, it is on the basis that (على أن) one-fifth is for Allah," i.e., it is established upon this foundation and governed by it. It could also be a conjunction to the first *anna* (أن), with the predicate of the first being omitted because the context indicates it; the estimation would be: "Know that whatever you take as spoils must be divided, so know that its fifth is for Allah." Alternatively, the *fa* (ف) may carry the sense of a conditional (الشرط), as the meaning ultimately boils down to: "If you take anything as spoils, then its fifth is for Allah," making the *fa* similar to the *fa* of apodosis (فاء الجزاء). The *anna* (أن) is repeated for emphasis (التأكيد). The original structure is: "Know that whatever you take as spoils, [know] that its fifth is for Allah." The primary object of knowledge is: "Whatever you take as spoils, its fifth is for Allah and the Apostle..." The Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة) was placed first for glorification (التعظيم).

**His saying:** «إِنْ كُنْتُمْ آمَنْتُمْ بِاللهِ» **"if you have faith in Allah..."** (8:41) is a condition for the command indicated by the beginning of the verse, meaning: "Pay its fifth if you have believed in Allah and what We sent down to Our servant." It has been suggested that it is connected to His saying in the previous verse: «فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللهَ مَوْلاكُمْ» ("then know that Allah is your Master"). However, the context, which is interrupted by the saying: «وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ» ("And know that whatever you take as spoils"), does not support this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ» **"and what We sent down to Our servant on the Day of Distinction (يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ) — *the day when truth was decisively separated from falsehood*..."** (8:41). It is apparent that what is meant is the Quran, given the specific mention of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding the "sending down" (الإنزال). If the meaning were the angels sent down on the day of Badr — as has been suggested — it would have been more appropriate, firstly, to say: "And *whom* (من) We sent down," or something conveying that meaning. Secondly, it would have said: "upon you" (عليكم) rather than "upon Our servant," for the angels were sent down to aid the believers alongside the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, as indicated by His saying: «فَاسْتَجابَ لَكُمْ أَنِّي مُمِدُّكُمْ بِأَلْفٍ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ مُرْدِفِينَ» ("...He answered you, ‘I will aid you with a thousand angels, following one another’") (8:9), and His saying thereafter: «إِذْ يُوحِي رَبُّكَ إِلَى الْمَلائِكَةِ أَنِّي مَعَكُمْ فَثَبِّتُوا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا» ("When your Lord revealed to the angels, ‘I am with you, so steady the faithful’") (8:12). Similar to these is His saying: «إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَلَنْ يَكْفِيَكُمْ أَنْ يُمِدَّكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ بِثَلاثَةِ آلافٍ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ مُنْزَلِينَ بَلى إِنْ تَصْبِرُوا وَتَتَّقُوا وَيَأْتُوكُمْ مِنْ فَوْرِهِمْ هذا يُمْدِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ بِخَمْسَةِ آلافٍ مِنَ الْمَلائِكَةِ مُسَوِّمِينَ» ("When you were saying to the faithful, ‘Is it not enough for you that your Lord should aid you with three thousand angels sent down? Yes, if you are steadfast and God-conscious, and they come at you in this heat of theirs, your Lord will aid you with five thousand marked angels’") (3:124-125).

In the transition (الالتفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person (التكلم) in His saying: «إِنْ كُنْتُمْ آمَنْتُمْ بِاللهِ وَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا» ("if you have faith in Allah and what We sent down to Our servant"), there is an unfolding of grace upon the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his selection for proximity that is not hidden.

It becomes clear through reflection on our previous discussion regarding His saying at the beginning of the Surah: «يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْأَنْفالِ قُلِ الْأَنْفالُ لِلَّهِ وَالرَّسُولِ» ("They ask you about the spoils. Say, ‘The spoils belong to Allah and the Apostle’") (8:1), that what is meant by His saying: «وَما أَنْزَلْنا عَلى عَبْدِنا يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ» ("and what We sent down to Our servant on the Day of Distinction") is His saying, Blessed and Exalted: «فَكُلُوا مِمَّا غَنِمْتُمْ حَلالاً طَيِّباً» ("So eat of what you have taken as spoils, such as is lawful and good") (8:69), along with the surrounding verses.

What is meant by **"the Day of Distinction" (يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ)** is the day of Badr, as evidenced by His subsequent saying: «يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ» **"the day the two hosts met"** (8:41). For the day of Badr was the day in which Allah distinguished between truth (الحق) and falsehood (الباطل); He vindicated the truth with His victory and nullified falsehood by forsaking it.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ» **"and Allah has power over all things"** (8:41) serves as a justification (التعليل) for His saying: "the Day of Distinction," regarding what it indicates of His distinguishing between truth and falsehood. It is as if it were said: "And Allah has power over all things, so He is able to distinguish between truth and falsehood in the way He did."

The meaning of the verse — and Allah knows best — is: "Know that one-fifth of whatever you take as spoils, of any kind, belongs to Allah, His Messenger, the kinsmen, the orphans, the needy, and the traveler. Therefore, return it to its rightful recipients if you have believed in Allah and what He sent down to His servant Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله on the day of Badr." This refers to the fact that the spoils (الأنفال) and the gains of war belong to Allah and His Messenger; no one shares them with Allah and His Messenger. However, Allah has permitted you to eat from them and allowed you to utilize them. Thus, He who allowed you to utilize them commands you to deliver their fifth to its rightful recipients.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that it contains an eternal legislation (تشريع مؤبد), as is the case with Quranic legislations. The ruling applies to whatever is called "spoils" (غنم) or "booty" (غنيمة), whether it be war booty taken from disbelievers or other things to which the term "spoils" applies linguistically, such as business profits, diving, seafaring, and what is extracted from treasures and minerals. Even if the specific occasion of revelation (مورد النزول) was war booty, the specific occasion does not restrict the general application.

Similarly, the apparent meaning of the categories of expenditure mentioned in His saying: «لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِي الْقُرْبى وَالْيَتامى وَالْمَساكِينِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ» ("one-fifth of it is for Allah and the Apostle, the kinsmen, the orphans, the needy, and the traveler") is that the categories are restricted to these groups. Each of them has a share in the sense of an independent right to receive that share, as is understood from the verse of Zakat, without the mention of these groups being merely illustrative (التمثيل).

All of this is beyond doubt when considering the primary meaning (المتبادر) of the verse's outward sense. Upon this, narrations have been transmitted through Shi'a channels from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام. The commentators among the Sunnis have differed in their interpretation of the verse, and we shall address their views in the following "Discussion of Narrations," Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِذْ أَنْتُمْ بِالْعُدْوَةِ الدُّنْيا وَهُمْ بِالْعُدْوَةِ الْقُصْوى وَالرَّكْبُ أَسْفَلَ مِنْكُمْ وَلَوْ تَواعَدْتُمْ لَاخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِي الْمِيعادِ وَلكِنْ لِيَقْضِيَ اللهُ أَمْراً كانَ مَفْعُولاً» **"When you were on the nearer side (الْعُدْوَةِ الدُّنْيا) and they were on the farther side (الْعُدْوَةِ الْقُصْوى), and the caravan was below you; and had you made a mutual appointment, you would surely have failed the appointment, but [it was] so that Allah might carry out a matter that was bound to be fulfilled..."** (8:42). *Al-Udwah* (العدوة) — with a *damma* or sometimes a *kasra* — is the bank or edge of a valley. *Al-Dunya* (الدنيا) is the feminine of *adna* (nearer), just as *al-Quswa* (القصوى) — sometimes said as *al-Qusya* — is the feminine of *aqsa* (farther). The **caravan (الركب)**, as has been said, refers to the camels carrying Abu Sufyan ibn Harb.

The adverbial phrase (الظرف) in His saying: «إِذْ أَنْتُمْ بِالْعُدْوَةِ» ("When you were on the side...") is a second clarification (بيان ثان) for His saying in the previous verse: "the Day of Distinction" (يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ), just as His saying: «يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ» ("the day the two hosts met") is the first clarification for it, connected to His saying: "We sent down to Our servant." As for what appears from some that it is a clarification for His saying: "and Allah has power over all things" based on what it provides according to the context — meaning: "Allah is powerful over your victory while you were lowly, when you were encamped at the nearer edge of the valley" — its far-fetched nature and the forced interpretation (التكلف) within it are not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَلَوْ تَواعَدْتُمْ لَاخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِي الْمِيعادِ» ("and had you made a mutual appointment, you would surely have failed the appointment") follows the preceding sentences that reveal the meeting of the two armies, the caravan being below them, and that Allah, by His power that overcomes everything, distinguished between truth and falsehood and supported truth against falsehood. Likewise, His subsequent saying: «وَلكِنْ لِيَقْضِيَ اللهُ أَمْراً كانَ مَفْعُولاً» ("but [it was] so that Allah might carry out a matter that was bound to be fulfilled"). All of this testifies that the meaning of His saying: «وَلَوْ تَواعَدْتُمْ لَاخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِي الْمِيعادِ» is to explain that the meeting in this manner occurred only by a specific will (مشية خاصة) from Allah, the Glorified. The polytheists, who possessed equipment and strength, encamped on the farther side, which had water and firm ground. The believers, despite their small number and humble state, were on the nearer side, which had no water and whose ground was sandy and unstable under their feet. The caravan escaped them as Abu Sufyan took the coastal route below. The two groups met with no barrier between them, and at that point, war was inevitable. Thus, the meeting and confrontation in this manner, followed by the believers' victory over the polytheists, did not arise from ordinary causes (أسباب عادية) but rather from a specific divine will through which His power appeared and His special care, victory, and support for the believers became manifest.

Thus, His saying: «وَلَوْ تَواعَدْتُمْ لَاخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِي الْمِيعادِ» is an explanation that this meeting was not by prior intent, resolve, deliberation, or consultation. For this reason, He followed it with His saying: «وَلكِنْ لِيَقْضِيَ اللهُ أَمْراً كانَ مَفْعُولاً» ("but [it was] so that Allah might carry out a matter that was bound to be fulfilled") with the corrective particle (الاستدراك).

His saying: «لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيى مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ» **"so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof (بَيِّنَةٍ) — *an evident and manifest sign of truth* — and he who lives might live by a clear proof"** (8:42) is to justify the matter that was decreed to be fulfilled. That is, Allah only decreed this meeting and confrontation that took place between you, followed by the support of the believers and the abandonment of the polytheists, so that it might be a manifest proof of the truth of truth and the falsehood of falsehood; thus, he who perishes does so after a clear proof, and he who lives does so after a clear proof.

By this, it becomes clear that what is meant by "perishing" and "living" is guidance (الهدى) and misguidance (الضلال), for that is what is linked to the existence of the manifest sign (الآية البينة).

Likewise, His saying: «وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَسَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ» **"and indeed Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing"** (8:42) is conjoined to His saying: "so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof..." That is, Allah only decreed what He decreed and did what He did because He is All-hearing, hearing your prayers, and All-knowing, knowing what is in your chests. In this is an allusion to what He mentioned at the beginning of the verses: «إِذْ تَسْتَغِيثُونَ رَبَّكُمْ فَاسْتَجابَ لَكُمْ» ("When you were pleading with your Lord for help, and He answered you...") (8:9) to the end of the verses.

According to this context — namely, to explain that the ultimate cause of the matter in this event was the specific divine decree (القضاء الخاص الإلهي) rather than ordinary causes — the subsequent sayings of the Exalted were framed: «إِذْ يُرِيكَهُمُ اللهُ فِي مَنامِكَ قَلِيلاً» ("When Allah showed them to you in your dream as few...") (8:43), and His saying: «وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ» ("And when Satan made their deeds seem fair to them...") (8:48), and His saying: «إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ» ("When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness said, ‘Their religion has deluded them’...") (8:49).

The meaning of the verse is: the Day of Distinction was the time when you were encamped on the nearer side and they were encamped on the farther side. Your encamping there and their encamping there coincided such that if you had made a mutual appointment to meet at this time, you would have disagreed and not met in this manner. Thus, it was not from you nor from them, but rather it was a matter already fulfilled, with Allah being its Decreer and Judge. He only decreed what He decreed so that a manifest sign might appear and the proof (الحجة) might be completed, and because He had thereby answered your call based on what He heard of your plea for help and what He knew of the need in your hearts.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِذْ يُرِيكَهُمُ اللهُ فِي مَنامِكَ قَلِيلاً» **"When Allah showed them to you in your dream as few..."** (8:43) to the end of the verse. **Failure (الفشل)** is weakness accompanied by alarm. **Contention (التنازع)** is disagreement; it is derived from *naz'* (النزع), a type of uprooting, as if each of the contenders is uprooting the other from his position. "Submission" (التسليم) is the result.

The speech implies the command "Remember," i.e., "Remember the time when Allah showed them to you in your dream as few." He only showed them to you as few to steady your hearts and reassure your souls. Had He shown them to you as many and you had mentioned it to the believers, weakness would have alarmed you and you would have disagreed regarding the matter of going out to face them. But He, the Exalted, saved you from failure and contention by showing them as few. Indeed, He is All-knowing of what is within the chests (ذات الصدور) — which are the hearts; He witnesses what rectifies the state of the hearts in their tranquility, steadfastness, and strength.

The verse indicates that Allah, the Glorified, showed His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله a glad-tidings vision in which he saw what Allah had promised him — that one of the two groups would be theirs — and He showed them as few, of no consequence. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله mentioned what he saw to the believers and gave them a promise of glad tidings, so they resolved to meet them. The proof for this is His saying: «وَلَوْ أَراكَهُمْ كَثِيراً لَفَشِلْتُمْ» ("and had He shown them to you as many, you would have surely lost heart..."), which is apparent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَإِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ الْتَقَيْتُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِكُمْ قَلِيلاً وَيُقَلِّلُكُمْ فِي أَعْيُنِهِمْ» **"And when He showed them to you, when you met, as few in your eyes, and He made you [appear] few in their eyes..."** (8:44) to the end of the verse. The meaning of the verse is clear. There is no contradiction between this verse and His saying, the Exalted: «قَدْ كانَ لَكُمْ آيَةٌ فِي فِئَتَيْنِ الْتَقَتا فِئَةٌ تُقاتِلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَأُخْرى كافِرَةٌ يَرَوْنَهُمْ مِثْلَيْهِمْ رَأْيَ الْعَيْنِ وَاللهُ يُؤَيِّدُ بِنَصْرِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ» ("There was certainly a sign for you in the two hosts that met: one host fighting in the way of Allah and the other faithless, who saw them as twice their own number with their own eyes. And Allah strengthens with His help whomever He wishes") (3:13), assuming that verse refers to the battle of Badr.

This is because the "making few" (التقليل) referred to in the verse under discussion is restricted by His saying: "when you met" (إِذِ الْتَقَيْتُمْ). By this, the contradiction is removed. It is as if Allah, the Glorified, showed the believers as few in the eyes of the polytheists at the very start of the meeting so that they would belittle their gathering, which would encourage them to fight and engage. Then, once they advanced and intermingled, the believers appeared numerous in their eyes, such that they saw them as twice their own number with their own eyes. Thereby, He weakened their resolve and made their hearts fly [in terror], resulting in the defeat. Thus, the verse in *al-Anfal* refers to the beginning of the encounter, while the verse in *Al 'Imran* refers to what happened after the advance and intermingling. His saying: «لِيَقْضِيَ اللهُ أَمْراً كانَ مَفْعُولاً» ("so that Allah might carry out a matter that was bound to be fulfilled") is connected to His saying: "He showed them to you" and serves as a justification for its content.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا لَقِيتُمْ فِئَةً فَاثْبُتُوا وَاذْكُرُوا اللهَ كَثِيراً لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ» **"O you who have faith! When you meet a host, remain steadfast (فَاثْبُتُوا) and remember Allah much, so that you may be felicitous"** (8:45) to the end of the three verses. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: **Steadfastness (الثبات)** — with a *fatha* on the *tha* — is the opposite of vanishing. End quote. In this context, it is the opposite of fleeing from the enemy. According to its meaning, it is more general than the **patience (الصبر)** He commands in His saying: «وَاصْبِرُوا إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ» ("and be patient; indeed Allah is with the patient"). For patience is steadfastness in the face of the disliked: with the heart, by not weakening, alarming, or despairing; and with the body, by not being lazy, negligent, moving from one's place, or rushing into what is not praiseworthy to rush into. Thus, patience is a specific type of steadfastness.

**Wind (الريح)**, as has been said, refers to might and statehood. Al-Raghib mentioned that "wind" in the verse means "victory" as a metaphor (استعارة), as if it is the nature of the wind to move what it blows upon, uprooting it and carrying it away. Victory over the enemy does to them what the wind does to something like dust, so it was used metaphorically for it.

Al-Raghib said: **Exultance (البطر)** is a bewilderment that afflicts a person due to poor endurance of a blessing, lack of fulfilling its right, and diverting it to other than its proper use. The Almighty said: «بَطَراً وَرِئاءَ النَّاسِ» ("exultantly and to be seen of people") and said: «بَطِرَتْ مَعِيشَتَها» ("exulted in its means of subsistence"). Its origin is *batirat ma'ishatuhu*, then the verb was diverted from it and it was made accusative. *Al-Batar* is close to *al-Tarab*, which is a lightness that mostly afflicts one from joy, though it may be said of grief. *Al-Baytarah* is the treatment of a beast. End quote. **Showing off (الرئاء)** is ostentation.

His saying: «فَاثْبُتُوا» ("remain steadfast") is a command for absolute steadfastness before the enemy and not fleeing from them; thus, it is not repeated by the second command for patience, as previously indicated.

His saying: «وَاذْكُرُوا اللهَ كَثِيراً» ("and remember Allah much") means in your hearts and with your tongues, for all of that is remembrance (ذكر). It is well-known that the internal states of the heart are what distinguish and specify a person's objectives, whether the spoken word matches them or not. For example, a poor person pleading with Allah for help from his poverty while saying "O All-Sufficient (يا غني)," or a sick person pleading with Him from his illness while saying "O Healer (يا شافي)." Even if the poor person said "O Allah" or the sick person said that, it would mean "O All-Sufficient" and "O Healer," because by the requirement of the state (مقتضى الحال) that prompted them to plead and call, they intend nothing but that, as is apparent.

He who goes out to fight his enemy, then meets him and the circumstances are prepared for battle — where there is nothing but the departing of souls, the shedding of blood, the loss of limbs, and everything that threatens a person with the annihilation of what he loves — his state transforms his thought and directs his will toward achieving what he desires through fighting and overcoming the enemy who threatens him with annihilation. He whose state is this and whose thinking is this only remembers Allah, the Glorified, in a way that suits his state and toward which his thought is directed.

This is the strongest evidence that what is meant by "remembering Allah much" is that the believer should remember what Allah has taught him of the gnosis (المعارف) related to this matter: that He, the Exalted, is his God and his Lord in whose hand is death and life, and He is indeed powerful over his victory; that He is his Master, an excellent Master and an excellent Helper; and that He has promised him victory when He said: «إِنْ تَنْصُرُوا اللهَ يَنْصُرْكُمْ وَيُثَبِّتْ أَقْدامَكُمْ» ("If you help Allah, He will help you and make your feet steady") and «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ» ("Indeed Allah does not allow the reward of the virtuous to go to waste"). Furthermore, the outcome of his matter in his fighting is one of the two best things: either victory over his enemy, raising the banner of Islam, and clearing the atmosphere for his religious felicity; or being killed in the way of Allah, transitioning through martyrdom to His mercy, entering the enclosure of His nobility, and neighboring His near-stationed friends (أولياؤه المقربون). This includes the rank of true gnosis that calls toward actual felicity and eternal nobility.

The remembrance was qualified as "much" so that the spirit of godwariness (التقوى) may be renewed whenever there appears to the person that which diverts his soul toward the love of the vanishing life, the enjoyment of the delusive ornaments of the world, and the psychological dangers that Satan casts through his enticement.

His saying: «وَأَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ» **"and obey Allah and His Apostle"** (8:46). The context suggests that what is meant is obedience to the obligations and instructions issued from the side of Allah and His Messenger regarding Jihad and the defense of the domain of religion and the core of Islam, as contained in the verses of Jihad and the Prophetic Sunnah — such as beginning by completing the proof, not harming women and children, refraining from attacking the enemy at night, and other rulings of Jihad.

His saying: «وَلا تَنازَعُوا فَتَفْشَلُوا وَتَذْهَبَ رِيحُكُمْ» **"and do not dispute, or you will lose heart and your power (رِيحُكُمْ) — *your strength, prestige, and victory* — will depart"** (8:46). That is, do not differ through contention among yourselves, lest that inherit the weakness of your will and the departure of your might, statehood, or victory; for the disagreement of opinions disrupts unity and weakens strength.

His saying: «وَاصْبِرُوا إِنَّ اللهَ مَعَ الصَّابِرِينَ» **"and be patient; indeed Allah is with the patient"** (8:46). That is, adhere to patience regarding the hardships of battle that the enemy threatens you with, regarding the frequent remembrance of Allah, and regarding the obedience to Allah and His Messenger, without being shaken by events, deterred by the weight of obedience, seduced by the pleasure of disobedience, or misled by self-conceit and pride.

The command for patience was emphasized by His saying: "indeed Allah is with the patient," because patience is the strongest aid against hardships and the firmest pillar against fluctuation in resolve and rapid shifts in will. It is what allows a person to engage in sound, tranquil thinking when confused notions and thoughts that weaken his will attack him from every side during terrors and calamities. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, is with the patient.

His saying: «وَلا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا مِنْ دِيارِهِمْ بَطَراً وَرِئاءَ النَّاسِ» **"Do not be like those who left their homes exultantly and to be seen of people..."** (8:47). This is a prohibition against adopting the way of these exultant, ostentatious people who bar others from the way of Allah. They are, as indicated by the context of the verses, the disbelievers of Quraysh. The attributes He mentioned — namely exultance, ostentation, and barring from the way of Allah — are what necessitated the prohibition of imitating them and adopting their way, as shown by the context. His saying: «وَاللهُ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ» **"and Allah encompasses what they do"** (8:47) informs of His encompassing their deeds and His authority and ownership over them. It is well-known that the implication of this is that their deeds are within His decree, proceeding by His permission and will. That which is of this nature is not something that can frustrate Allah, the Glorified. The sentence is like an allusion to what He explicitly states several verses later: «وَلا يَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَبَقُوا إِنَّهُمْ لا يُعْجِزُونَ» ("Let the faithless not suppose that they have outmaneuvered [Us]. Indeed they cannot frustrate [Us]") (8:59).

It is clear that including these qualifications — namely His saying: "exultantly and to be seen of people and barring from the way of Allah" — makes the prohibition apply to them. The estimation is: "Do not go out from your homes to fight the enemies of religion exultantly and ostentatiously with worldly finery, barring people from the way of Allah by inviting them through your words and deeds to abandon the godwariness of Allah, to immerse themselves in His disobediences, and to strip themselves of obedience to His commands and instructions; for that frustrates your deeds, extinguishes the light of faith, and nullifies its effect upon your collective. There is no way to the success of effort and the attainment of important goals except the even path (سوي الصراط) paved by the upright religion and made easy by the primordial faith (الملة الفطرية), and Allah does not guide the transgressing folk to their corrupt goals."

The three verses have included six matters that Allah, the Glorified, made obligatory for the believers to observe in Islamic wars upon encounter: steadfastness, remembering Allah much, obeying Allah and His Messenger, avoiding contention, and not going out exultantly and ostentatiously while barring from the way of Allah.

The sum of these six matters is a comprehensive military instruction (دستور حربي جامع) that lacks nothing of the essentials of military instructions. Careful reflection on the details of events in the history of Islamic wars that occurred in the time of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله — such as Badr, Uhud, al-Khandaq, Hunayn, and others — clarifies that the matter of victory and defeat revolved around the Muslims' observance of the articles of this divine instruction or their lack thereof, their vigilance over it or their negligence in it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ وَقالَ لا غالِبَ لَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ» **"And when Satan made their deeds seem fair to them and said, ‘No one shall overcome you today...’"** (8:48) to the end of the verse. Satan's beautifying (تزيين) a person's deed for him is his casting into his heart that the deed is good and beautiful, to be relished. This is done by agitating his internal powers and inner emotions related to that deed, so his heart is attracted to it and he finds no room to realize its evil effect and wretched consequence.

It is not far-fetched that His saying: "and said, ‘No one shall overcome you today...’" is an interpretation or like an interpretation of the Satanic beautification, such that what is meant by "deeds" are their results — namely the strength, weapons, and equipment they prepared, the singing girls, musical instruments, and wine they brought out, and the military order and led horses they displayed. It is also possible that what is meant is the deeds themselves — namely the types of their persistence in transgression and misguidance, their insistence on opposing Allah and His Messenger, and their indulgence in injustice and profligacy. Thus, his reported saying: "No one among the people shall overcome you today" would be that which completes Satan's beautification and reassures their souls regarding their concern with fighting the Muslims. He completed this by saying: «وَإِنِّي جارٌ لَكُمْ» **"and indeed I am your neighbor (جارٌ) — *your protector and ally who will defend you*."**

Neighboring (الجوار) was among the customs of the Arabs in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية), who lived a tribal life. Among the rights of neighboring was the neighbor's support for the neighbor if an enemy attacked him, and it has various effects according to the customs prevalent in human societies.

His saying: «فَلَمَّا تَراءَتِ الْفِئَتانِ نَكَصَ عَلى عَقِبَيْهِ» **"But when the two hosts sighted each other, he took to his heels (نَكَصَ عَلى عَقِبَيْهِ) — *he turned back in retreat*..."** (8:48). *Al-Nukus* (النكوص) is to recoil from something, and "on his heels" is a circumstantial qualifier (حال). The **heel (العقب)** is the back of the foot; i.e., he recoiled and retreated backwards in defeat.

His saying: «إِنِّي أَرى ما لا تَرَوْنَ» **"‘Indeed I see what you do not see...’"** (8:48) is a justification for his saying: "Indeed I am quit of you." Perhaps it is an allusion to the descent of the angels following one another, with whom Allah supported the Muslims. Likewise, His saying: «إِنِّي أَخافُ اللهَ وَاللهُ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ» **"‘...Indeed I fear Allah, and Allah is severe in retribution’"** (8:48) is a justification for his saying: "Indeed I am quit of you" and an interpretation of the previous justification.

The meaning is: And the Day of Distinction was the time when Satan beautified for the polytheists what they were doing to oppose Allah and His Messenger and fight the believers, and what they were engaged in to prepare to extinguish the light of Allah. He beautified that in their eyes and reassured their souls by saying: "No one among the people shall overcome you today, and I am your protector, defending you." But when the two hosts sighted each other — the polytheists seeing the believers and the believers seeing the polytheists — Satan retreated backwards in defeat and said to the polytheists: "Indeed I am quit of you; indeed I see what you do not see of the descent of the angels of victory for the believers and the punishment they have which threatens you; indeed I fear the punishment of Allah, and Allah is severe in retribution."

This meaning — as you see — is capable of being applied to Satan's whispering (وسوسة) to them in their hearts, agitating them against the believers, encouraging them to fight them, and reassuring their souls with what they had prepared. Then, when the two hosts sighted each other, victory descended, and terror seized their hearts, their delusions were reversed, their thoughts changed, and the claim of victory and the wish for conquest and triumph turned into a dominant fear in their souls and a disappointment and despair encompassing their hearts.

It is also capable of being applied to a Satanic representation (تصور شيطاني) that appeared to them, to which their senses were attracted, such that he appeared to them in the form of a human and said to them what Allah recounted of his saying: "No one among the people shall overcome you today, and I am your neighbor." Thus, he misled them, marched them, and brought them close to the battle. Then, when the two hosts drew near and sighted each other, and he saw the situation contrary to what he had hoped and craved, he took to his heels and said: "Indeed I am quit of you; indeed I see what you do not see of the descent of victory and the angels; indeed I fear Allah, and Allah is severe in retribution." Narrations of the story from both Shi'a and Sunni channels have been transmitted that support this view.

Namely, that Satan appeared to the polytheists in the form of Suraqah ibn Malik ibn Ju'shum al-Kinani, then al-Mudliji, who was among the nobles of Kinanah. He said to them what he said and carried their banner until the two groups met, then he fled in defeat saying: "Indeed I am quit of you; indeed I see what you do not see..." to the end of what Allah the Exalted recounted. The narration will come in the following "Discussion of Narrations," Allah willing.

Some commentators have insisted on the first view and rejected the second by disparaging the transmitted reports and weakening the chains of the narrations. While they are not mutawatir (متواترة) — *narrated by so many that they could not have conspired on a lie* — nor surrounded by certain definitive clues that necessitate complete trust, the core meaning is not among the impossibilities that sound reason rejects, nor among the stories rejected by authentic reports. There is no barrier to Satan representing himself to them, leading them to the place of misguidance and transgression, until what he desired was completed for him, he left them in their destruction, or until he witnessed a divine punishment and took to his heels in flight.

Furthermore, the context of the noble verse is closer to providing this second view than the first, especially considering His saying: "and indeed I am your neighbor," and His saying: "But when the two hosts sighted each other, he took to his heels," and His saying: "Indeed I see what you do not see." For reducing the meaning of His saying: "Indeed I see," for example, to psychological notions through a type of metaphorical treatment is very far-fetched.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ» **"When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness said, ‘Their religion has deluded them’..."** (8:49) to the end of the verse. That is, the hypocrites — those who displayed faith and concealed disbelief — and those in whose hearts is a sickness — the weak in faith whose souls are not free from doubt and suspicion — said, pointing to the believers with a gesture of belittlement and humiliation: "Their religion has deluded these people; for were it not for the delusion of their religion, they would not have ventured into this manifest destruction, being a humiliated small band with no equipment or numbers, while Quraysh possess such equipment, strength, and power."

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَإِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ» **"But whoever puts his trust in Allah, then Allah is indeed All-mighty, All-wise"** (8:49) is in the position of answering their statement and exposing their own delusion. His saying: "then Allah is indeed All-mighty, All-wise" is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect. The meaning is: "These hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness were mistaken in their statement, for the believers put their trust in Allah, attributed the reality of influence to Him, and joined themselves to His power and strength. Whoever entrusts his matter to Allah, Allah suffices him, because He is All-mighty, aiding whoever seeks His aid, and All-wise, not failing to place every matter in its appropriate place."

In the verse is evidence of the presence of a group of hypocrites and those weak in faith at Badr when the two hosts met.

As for the hypocrites — those who displayed Islam and concealed disbelief — there is no sense in them being among the polytheists; thus, they were only among the Muslims. However, the question remains regarding the factor that necessitated their steadfastness on a day that was so severe.

As for those weak in faith or those doubting the reality of Islam, it is possible that they were among the believers or in the host of the polytheists. It has been said that they were a group from Quraysh who had accepted Islam in Mecca, but their fathers detained them and they were forced to go out with the polytheists to Badr. When they were present and witnessed the small number and lowly state of the Muslims, they said: "Poor people, their religion has deluded them." This will come in the following "Discussion of Narrations," Allah willing.

In any case, one should carefully examine the research regarding what this verse provides of the presence of a group of hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness on the day of Badr during the fighting. One should extract the reality of the cause that necessitated these hypocrites and weak ones to attend this battle and stand in that difficult, terrifying position which ordinary causes do not support, and in which only the men of truth whose hearts Allah has tested for faith would stand. Why did they attend? How and why did they remain patient with the patient ones of the host of Islam? Perhaps we will be successful in some research on that in what will come of the verses of Surah al-Tawbah regarding the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness, Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَلَوْ تَرى إِذْ يَتَوَفَّى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْمَلائِكَةُ» **"Were you to see when the angels take the souls of the faithless..."** (8:50) to the completion of the two verses. *Al-Tawaffi* (التوفي) is to take a right in its entirety. It is used frequently in His speech to mean the seizing of the soul. The attribution of seizing their souls to the angels — despite what is in some verses of its attribution to the Angel of Death, and in others to Allah the Glorified, such as His saying: «قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ» ("Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls’") (32:11), and His saying: «اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها» ("Allah takes the souls at the time of their death") (39:42) — is evidence that the Angel of Death has assistants who undertake the seizing of souls. They are like the working hands for him, proceeding by his permission and acting by his command, just as he proceeds by permission from Allah and acts by command from Him. Thus, it is correct to attribute the taking of souls to the assistant angels, to the Angel of Death, and to Allah the Glorified.

His saying: «يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبارَهُمْ» **"striking their faces and their backs"** (8:50). Its apparent meaning is that they strike the fronts of their bodies and the opposite of that; thus, it is an allusion to their encompassing them and covering all their directions with striking. It has been said that "backs" (الأدبار) is an allusion to the buttocks; by association, what is meant by "faces" would be the front of their heads. Striking the faces and backs in this sense is intended for contempt and humiliation.

His saying: «وَذُوقُوا عَذابَ الْحَرِيقِ» **"‘and taste the punishment of the burning’"** (8:50). That is, the angels say to them: "Taste the punishment of the burning," which is the Fire.

His saying: «ذلِكَ بِما قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ» **"‘That is because of what your hands have sent ahead...’"** (8:51) is a completion of their reported speech or an allusion to the sum of what is done to them and what the angels say to them. The meaning is: "We only make you taste the punishment of the burning because of what your hands have sent ahead," or: "We strike your faces and backs and make you taste the punishment of the burning because of what your hands have sent ahead."

His saying: «وَأَنَّ اللهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ» **"‘...and because Allah is not unjust to [His] servants’"** (8:51) is conjoined to the position of His saying: "because of what [your hands] have sent ahead." That is: "And that is because Allah is not unjust to the servants," meaning He does not wrong any of His servants, for He, the Exalted, is upon a straight path; there is no failure or inconsistency in His act. If He were to wrong one person, He would wrong everyone, and if He were a wrongdoer, He would be "most unjust" (ظلام) to the servants. So understand that.

The context of the verses testifies that what is meant by these whom Allah describes as the angels taking their souls and punishing them are those killed at Badr from the polytheists of Quraysh.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ» **"[Their way is] like the way of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them: they defied the signs of Allah..."** (8:52) to the end of the verse. **Way (الدأب)** and **custom (الديدن)**: the habit, which is the work that persists and upon which a person proceeds, and the path he treads. The meaning is: the disbelief of these people resembles the disbelief of the clan of Pharaoh and those before them of the past disbelieving nations; they disbelieved in the signs of Allah and sinned thereby, so Allah seized them for their sins. Indeed, Allah is All-strong, not weakening in seizing them, severe in retribution when He seizes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ» **"That is because Allah never changes a blessing He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls..."** (8:53) etc. That is, the retribution with which Allah, the Glorified, punishes only follows a prior divine blessing by stripping it and replacing it. No blessing among the divine blessings vanishes, nor is it replaced by vengeance and retribution, except with the change of its locus, which is the human souls. For the blessing He bestowed upon a people was only poured upon them because they were prepared for it in their souls. They are not stripped of it, nor is it replaced for them by vengeance and retribution, except because of their changing what is in their souls of preparation and the criterion for the outpouring, and their becoming clothed with the preparation for retribution.

This is a universal rule (ضابط كلي) regarding the transformation of blessing into vengeance and retribution. More comprehensive than it is His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُغَيِّرُ ما بِقَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ» ("Indeed Allah does not change a people’s lot unless they change what is in their souls") (13:11), even if its apparent sense is more clearly applicable to the transformation of blessing into vengeance.

In any case, His saying: "That is because Allah never changes..." etc., is of the nature of justifying by a general matter and applying it to its specific instance. That is, the seizing of the polytheists of Quraysh for their sins, their punishment with this severe retribution, and the replacement of Allah's blessing upon them with severe retribution is but a branch of the branches of a persistent divine precedent (سنة جارية إلهية), which is that Allah does not change a blessing He bestowed upon a people until they change what is in their souls.

His saying: «وَأَنَّ اللهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ» **"and that Allah is All-hearing, All-knowing"** (8:53) is another justification after the justification by His saying: "That is because Allah never changes..." etc. Its apparent meaning — by the indication of the context — is: "And that is because Allah is All-hearing of your prayers, All-knowing of your needs; He heard your plea for help and knew of your need, so He answered you and punished your enemies who disbelieved in the signs of Allah." It is also possible that what is meant is: "That is because Allah is All-hearing of their words, All-knowing of their deeds, so He punished them for that." It is possible to combine the two possibilities.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَهْلَكْناهُمْ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ» **"[Their way is] like the way of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them: they denied the signs of their Lord, so We destroyed them for their sins..."** (8:54) etc. He repeated the previous comparison due to the similarity of the case with what preceded. Thus, His saying: "like the way of Pharaoh’s clan..." etc., the first time, is a comparison for His saying: "That is because of what your hands have sent ahead and because Allah is not unjust to the servants." Similarly, His saying: "like the way of Pharaoh’s clan — to His saying — and all of them were wrongdoers," the second time, is a comparison for His saying: "That is because Allah never changes a blessing..." etc.

However, the second comparison includes a type of transition (الالتفات) in His saying: "so We destroyed them for their sins." In its place in the first comparison was: "so Allah seized them for their sins," without transition. Perhaps the reason for this is that the second comparison was preceded by the information that Allah is the Bestower of blessings upon His servants and does not change them except due to their changing what is in their souls. This is the state of the Lord (الرب) toward His servants. This necessitated considering these people as servants who did not proceed upon the path of servitude to their Lord. Therefore, He changed some of the context of the comparison, saying in the second: "they denied the signs of their Lord," whereas in its place in the first was His saying: "they defied the signs of Allah." For this reason, He transitioned here from the third person to the first person plural, saying: "so We destroyed them for their sins," to indicate that He, the Glorified, is their Lord and He is their Destroyer. He used the first person plural (المتكلم مع الغير) to indicate the greatness of the matter and the majesty of the station, and that He has intermediaries who act by His command and proceed by His will.

His saying: «وَأَغْرَقْنا آلَ فِرْعَوْنَ» **"and We drowned Pharaoh’s clan"** (8:54). He made the object explicit and did not say "and We drowned them," to avoid ambiguity regarding the pronoun returning to the clan of Pharaoh and those before them all together.

His saying, the Exalted: «وَكُلٌّ كانُوا ظالِمِينَ» **"and all of them were wrongdoers (ظالِمِينَ) — *unjust to themselves and others by violating the rights of Allah*"** (8:54). That is, all of these whom the divine punishment seized — from the disbelievers of Quraysh, the clan of Pharaoh, and those before them — were wrongdoers in the side of Allah.

In this is an explanation that Allah, the Glorified, does not seize anyone with His severe retribution, nor does He replace His blessing upon anyone with vengeance, unless he is a wrongdoer with a wrongdoing that replaces the blessing of Allah with disbelief in His signs. Thus, He only punishes with His punishment those who deserve it.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In *al-Kafi*, narrated from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from al-Husayn ibn 'Uthman, from Sama'ah, who said: "I asked Abu al-Hasan عليه‌السلام about the one-fifth (الخمس), and he said: 'It is in everything that people benefit from, whether little or much.'"

Also in it, from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Hammad ibn 'Isa, from some of our companions, from the Righteous Servant (العبد الصالح) [Imam al-Kazim] عليه‌السلام, who said: "The one-fifth is in five things: from spoils (الغنائم), diving (الغوص), treasures (الكنوز), minerals (المعادن), and seafaring (الملاحة) — the one-fifth is taken from all these categories — and it is placed for those for whom Allah placed it. Four-fifths are divided among those who fought for it and took charge of it.

The one-fifth is divided among them into six shares: a share for Allah, a share for His Messenger, a share for the kinsmen, a share for the orphans, a share for the needy, and a share for the travelers. The share of Allah and the share of the Messenger belong to the holders of authority (أولي الأمر) after the Messenger of Allah by inheritance. Thus, he has three shares: two shares by inheritance and one share allotted to him by Allah. So he has half of the one-fifth in total. The second half of the one-fifth is among his household: a share for their orphans, a share for their needy, and a share for their travelers. It is divided among them according to the Book and the Sunnah — that which suffices them for their year. If anything remains of it, it belongs to the ruler (الوالي). If it is insufficient or falls short of their sufficiency, it is upon the ruler to spend from his own [funds] what they need to be sufficient. He is obliged to maintain them because he receives what remains from them. Allah only made this one-fifth specifically for them — excluding the needy and travelers among the [general] people — as a substitute for them for the charities (صدقات) of the people, as a purification from Allah — due to their kinship to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله — and a nobility from Allah for them from the filth of the people. So He made for them specifically from Himself that which suffices them, so as not to place them in a position of humiliation and poverty. There is no harm in some of them giving charity to others.

These for whom Allah made the one-fifth are the kinsmen of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله whom Allah mentioned when He said: «وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ» ("And warn your nearest relations"). They are the children of Abd al-Muttalib themselves, male and female. There is no one among them from the [other] households of Quraysh or from the Arabs. Neither are their clients (موالي) among them nor included in this one-fifth. The charities of the people may be lawful for their clients, and they and the [general] people are equal.

Whoever's mother is from Banu Hashim and whose father is from the rest of Quraysh — charities are lawful for him, and he has nothing of the one-fifth, because Allah says: «ادْعُوهُمْ لِآبائِهِمْ» ("Call them by their fathers' names")."

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain to Ali ibn Mahziyar, who said: "Ali ibn Rashid said to me: 'I said to him [the Imam]: You commanded me to take charge of your affairs and take your right — so I informed your clients of that — and some of them said to me: And what is his right? I did not know what to answer him!' He said: 'The one-fifth is obligatory upon them.' I said: 'In what thing?' He said: 'In their goods and their estates.' I said: 'And the merchant is liable, and the craftsman with his hand?' He said: 'That is when it is possible for them after their maintenance (مئونتهم).'"

Also in it, with its chain to Zakariyya ibn Malik al-Ju'fi, from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام: that he was asked about the saying of Allah: «وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ ـ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِي الْقُرْبى ـ وَالْيَتامى وَالْمَساكِينِ وَابْنِ السَّبِيلِ». He said: "The fifth of Allah, Mighty and Majestic, is for the Imam; the fifth of the Messenger is for the Imam; the fifth of the kinsmen is for the kinship of the Messenger, for the Imam. The orphans are the orphans of the family of the Messenger, the needy are from them, and the travelers are from them. It does not go out from them to others."

Also in it, with its chain to Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Abi Nasr, from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "Ibrahim ibn Abi al-Bilad said to him: 'Is Zakat obligatory upon you?' He said: 'No, but there is surplus and we give like this.' And he was asked about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: «وَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما غَنِمْتُمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ ـ فَأَنَّ لِلَّهِ خُمُسَهُ وَلِلرَّسُولِ وَلِذِي الْقُرْبى». It was said to him: 'That which was for Allah, for whom is it?' He said: 'For the Messenger.' 'And that which was for the Messenger?' 'It is for the Imam.' It was said: 'What if one category is more than another, and one category is less than another?' He said: 'That is for the Imam.' It was said: 'How did the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله do?' He said: 'He only gave according to what he saw [fit], and likewise the Imam.'"

**I say:** The narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام are mutawatir (متواترة) regarding the restriction of the one-fifth to Allah, His Messenger, the Imam from his household, and the orphans, needy, and travelers of his kinship; it does not exceed them to others. It is divided into six shares as mentioned in the narrations. It is not restricted to war booty but encompasses everything called "spoils" (غنيمة) linguistically, including business profits, treasures, diving, minerals, and seafaring. In their narrations — as preceded — this is a gift from Allah to the Ahl al-Bayt because He forbade them Zakat and charities.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from another route from Ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with both): that Najdah al-Haruri sent to ask him about the share of the kinsmen — whom Allah mentioned. He wrote to him: "We used to see that we were them, but our people refused that for us and said: 'And to whom do you see it [should go]?'" Ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with both) said: "It is for the kinsmen of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله — the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله divided it for them.

Umar (may Allah be pleased with him) had offered us an offer from that — we saw it as less than our right, so we returned it to him and refused to accept it." He had offered them to help their marrying ones, to pay for their debtors (غارمهم), and to give to their poor, but he refused to give them more than that.

**I say:** His saying in the narration: "They said: 'To whom do you see it?'" means: those whom Najdah al-Haruri sent said to Ibn Abbas: "And Najdah says: To whom do you see the one-fifth [should go]?" i.e., he asks you for your fatwa (فتواك) on who should receive the one-fifth.

His saying: "It is for the kinsmen of the Messenger of Allah, he divided it for them..." etc. Its apparent sense is that he interpreted "the kinsmen" (ذي القربى) as the relatives of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The apparent sense of the previous narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام is that they interpreted "the kinsmen" as the Imam from the Ahl al-Bayt. The apparent sense of the verse supports this, as it used the singular form (لفظ المفرد)!

In the same source, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from Abd al-Rahman ibn Abi Layla, who said: "I asked Ali (رضي الله عنه), saying: 'O Commander of the Faithful, inform me how Abu Bakr and Umar (رضي الله عنهما) acted regarding the *khums* (الخمس) — *the one-fifth tax levied on certain types of wealth* — which is your portion?' He replied: 'As for Abu Bakr (رضي الله عنه), there were no *khums* during his governorship. As for Umar (رضي الله عنه), he continued to hand it over to me from every *khums*, until the *khums* of Susa and Jundisabur arrived. While I was with him, he said: "This is your portion, the People of the House (أهل البيت), from the *khums*. However, a situation has befallen some of the Muslims and their need has become severe." I said: "Yes." Then al-Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib jumped up and said: "Do not interfere in what belongs to us." I said: "Are we not among the most lenient of the Muslims?" So the Commander of the Faithful (Umar) took possession of it, and by Allah, we did not take possession of it, nor was I able to do so during the governorship of Uthman (رضي الله عنه).'"

Then Ali (رضي الله عنه) began to speak, saying: "Indeed, Allah forbade charity (الصدقة) — *alms given for the sake of Allah* — to His Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so He compensated him with a share of the *khums* in place of what He forbade him. And He forbade it specifically to his People of the House (أهل البيت) to the exclusion of his community (الأمة), so He assigned them a share along with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) as compensation for what He forbade them."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Abbas (رضي الله عنهما) that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "I have turned you away from the washings of hands (غسالة الأيدي) — *a metaphor for the impurities of people's wealth given as charity* — because you have in the fifth of the *khums* (خمس الخمس) that which suffices you or is enough for you."

**I say:** This is based on the view that the share of the People of the House is only that which belongs to the near of kin (ذو القربى).

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from Jubayr ibn Mut'im (رضي الله عنه) who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) divided the share of the near of kin (ذو القربى) between the Banu Hashim and the Banu al-Muttalib. I and Uthman ibn Affan walked until we entered upon him and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, these are your brothers from the Banu Hashim—their merit is not denied due to the position Allah has granted you among them. But what of our brothers from the Banu al-Muttalib? You have given to them and excluded us, yet we and they are of the same rank in lineage?' He replied: 'They did not separate from us in the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) nor in Islam (الإسلام).'" In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Zayd ibn Arqam (رضي الله عنه) who said: "The family of Muhammad (آل محمد) who were given the *khums* are the family of Ali, the family of Abbas, the family of Ja'far, and the family of Aqil."

**I say:** The narrations in this chapter are numerous from the Sunni (أهل السنة) chains. The narrations recounting the practice of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from their chains have differed; some imply that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to divide the *khums* into four shares, while others imply a division into five shares.

However, what is nearly certain (المسلم) among them is that some shares of the *khums* are specific to the relatives of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and they are the ones intended by 'the near of kin' (ذو القربى) in the verse of *khums*, contrary to what is found in the narrations reported from the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام).

Also nearly certain among them is that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to divide it among the descendants of al-Muttalib as long as he was alive, and that it was cut off from them in this capacity during the time of the three Caliphs, and the matter continued as such after them.

It is also certain among them that the *khums* is specific to the spoils of war (غنائم الحرب) — contrary to the narrations from the chains of the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام) — and does not extend to everything that falls under the linguistic definition of 'spoil' (الغنيمة).

What pertains to the verse from the summary of the exegetical research is what we have already presented. There are other theological (كلامية) or legal (فقهية) discussions that lie outside our purpose. There is also a socio-legal (حقوقي اجتماعي) discussion regarding the impact of *khums* on Islamic society, which will come to you during the discussion on *zakat* (الزكاة).

There remains the discussion regarding what the narrations contain: that Allah, the Glorified, intended by the legislation of *khums* to honor the People of the House (أهل بيت) of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his family, and to elevate them above taking the 'filth of the people' (أوساخ الناس) from their wealth. It appears that this is derived from the saying of the Exalted in the verse of *zakat*, addressing His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **خُذْ مِنْ أَمْوالِهِمْ صَدَقَةً تُطَهِّرُهُمْ وَتُزَكِّيهِمْ بِها وَصَلِّ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ صَلاتَكَ سَكَنٌ لَهُمْ** "Take charity from their possessions to cleanse them and purify them thereby, and bless them. Indeed your blessing is a comfort to them" (9:103). For cleansing (التطهير) and purification (التزكية) only relate to that which is not free of grime, filth, and the like, whereas nothing has occurred in the verse of *khums* that suggests this.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn Jarir narrated from Urwah ibn al-Zubayr (رضي الله عنه) who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to fight in verses of the Quran. The first battle the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) witnessed was Badr. The leader of the polytheists (المشركين) that day was Utbah ibn Rabi'ah ibn Abd Shams. They met on Friday at Badr, seventeen or sixteen nights having passed of Ramadan. The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) were three hundred and some odd men, while the polytheists were between one thousand and nine hundred. That was the Day of the Criterion (يوم الفرقان) — *the day Allah distinguished between truth and falsehood*. The first person killed that day was Mihja', the client (مولى) of Amr, a man from the Ansar (الأنصار). Allah defeated the polytheists that day; more than seventy of them were killed, and a similar number were taken captive."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib (رضي الله عنه) who said: "The Night of the Criterion (ليلة الفرقان) was the day the two hosts met in its morning—the night of Friday, the seventeenth of Ramadan."

**I say:** Similar to this has been narrated by Ibn Jarir from al-Hasan ibn Ali, and by Ibn Abi Shaybah from Ja'far from his father. Also from him, from Abu Bakr from Abd al-Rahman ibn Hisham, and from him from Amir ibn Rabi'ah al-Badri: the same, but it mentions that the Day of Badr was Monday, the seventeenth of Ramadan.

In some reports from the Imams of the People of the House (عليه السلام), the nineteenth of Ramadan is sometimes called 'the day the two hosts met' because its night is counted in their reports as the Night of Ordainment (ليلة القدر). This is a different meaning from what is intended in the verse by **يَوْمَ الْفُرْقانِ يَوْمَ الْتَقَى الْجَمْعانِ** "the Day of Distinction, the day the two hosts met." In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Ishaq ibn Ammar, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: "On the nineteenth of the month of Ramadan, the two hosts meet." I asked: "What is the meaning of His saying: 'the two hosts meet'?" He replied: "In it is gathered what He intends—of His bringing forward, His delaying, His will, and His decree."

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Muhammad ibn Yahya, from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) regarding His saying: **وَالرَّكْبُ أَسْفَلَ مِنْكُمْ** "and the caravan was below you" (8:42), he said: "Abu Sufyan and his companions."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **لِيَهْلِكَ مَنْ هَلَكَ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ وَيَحْيى مَنْ حَيَّ عَنْ بَيِّنَةٍ** "so that he who perishes might perish by a clear proof, and he who lives might live by a clear proof" (8:42), he said: "He (the Imam) said: 'So that he who remains knows that Allah aided him.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِذْ يُرِيكُمُوهُمْ إِذِ الْتَقَيْتُمْ** "And when He showed them to you when you met" (8:44), Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Jarir, Abu al-Shaykh, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Mas'ud (رضي الله عنه) who said: "They were made to appear few in our eyes on the day of Badr, until I said to a man beside me: 'Do you see them as seventy?' He said: 'No, rather a hundred.'"

In the same source, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا لَقِيتُمْ** "O you who have faith! When you meet..." (8:45), al-Hakim narrated and authenticated from Abu Musa (رضي الله عنه) that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to dislike raising the voice during combat.

In the same source, Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from al-Nu'man ibn Muqarrin (رضي الله عنه) who said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was at battle, he would not fight at the beginning of the day, but would delay it until the sun had passed its zenith, the winds blew, and victory descended."

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ** "And when Satan made their deeds seem fair to them" (8:48), with its chain to Yahya ibn al-Hasan ibn Furat, who said: "Abu al-Muqaddam Tha'labah ibn Zayd al-Ansari narrated to us, saying: 'I heard Jabir ibn Abdillah ibn Haram al-Ansari (رحمه الله) say: "Iblis (إبليس) — *the primary devil and tempter of humanity* — manifested in four forms: He manifested on the day of Badr in the form of Suraqah ibn Malik ibn Jush'um al-Mudliji, saying to the Quraysh: 'No one shall overcome you today from among the people, and I am your protector.' But when the two hosts sighted each other, he turned back on his heels and said: 'Indeed I am quit of you.'

He manifested on the Day of Aqaba in the form of Munabbih ibn al-Hajjaj, calling out: 'Muhammad and the apostates (الصباة) with him are at al-Aqaba, so overtake them!' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to the Ansar: 'Do not fear, for his voice shall not go beyond him.'

He manifested on the day the Quraysh gathered in the Assembly House (دار الندوة) in the form of an old man from the people of Najd, and he advised them in their affair. Then Allah the Exalted revealed: **وَإِذْ يَمْكُرُ بِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِيُثْبِتُوكَ أَوْ يَقْتُلُوكَ أَوْ يُخْرِجُوكَ وَيَمْكُرُونَ وَيَمْكُرُ اللهُ وَاللهُ خَيْرُ الْماكِرِينَ** 'When the faithless plotted against you to take you captive, or to kill you, or to expel you. They plotted and Allah devised, and Allah is the best of devisers' (8:30).

And he manifested on the day of the passing of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the form of al-Mughirah ibn Shu'bah, saying: 'O people, do not make it a Persian (كسروانية) or Roman (قيصرانية) kingship; expand it and it will expand, and do not return it to the Banu Hashim, lest the pregnant women look toward it (in expectation).'"

In *al-Majma'*, it is said: "When they met, Iblis was in the ranks of the polytheists. Al-Harith ibn Hisham took him by the hand, but he turned back on his heels. Al-Harith ibn Hisham said to him: 'O Suraqah, where to? Do you desert us in this state?' He replied: 'Indeed I see what you do not see.' Al-Harith said: 'By Allah, we see nothing but the weaklings (جعاميس) of Yathrib.' Then he pushed al-Harith in the chest and departed, and the people were defeated.

When they returned to Mecca, they said: 'Suraqah defeated the people.' This reached Suraqah, and he said: 'By Allah, I was not even aware of your march until I heard of your defeat.' They said: 'You came to us on such-and-such a day,' but he swore to them (that he had not). When they embraced Islam, they realized that it had been Satan." He said: "And that was narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام)."

**I say:** Ibn Shahrashub narrated the same from them (عليه السلام). Regarding the meaning of these two narrations, there are many reports from the Sunni chains from Ibn Abbas and others.

In the preceding commentary (البيان), it was mentioned that some exegetes found this far-fetched and weakened the narrations concerning it. However, these narrations only establish a matter that is possible and not impossible. Mere far-fetchedness (الاستبعاد) is not something to be relied upon in scholarly research. Imaginal manifestations (التمثلات البرزخية) are not rare or anomalous, so there is no reason to insist on denial, just as there is no reason for insistence on affirmation, except that the apparent meaning of the verse is more in accord with affirmation.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَإِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ** "And when Satan made fair to them..." (8:48-49), Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn Ishaq regarding the saying: **إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ** "When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness said..." (8:49), he said: "They were a group who went out with the Quraysh; their fathers had held them back, so they went out while they were in doubt. When they saw the small number of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), they said: 'Their religion has deluded these people,' when they saw what they had come upon—their small number and the large number of their enemy."

They were a group from the Quraysh, five of whom are named: Qays ibn al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah, Abu Qays ibn al-Fakih ibn al-Mughirah (both from the Makhzum tribe), al-Harith ibn Zam'ah, Ali ibn Umayyah ibn Khalaf, and al-Asi ibn Munabbih.

**I say:** This is applicable in a sense to the saying of the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ** "and those in whose hearts is a sickness" only. In some commentaries, it is said that those who said: **غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ** "Their religion has deluded these people" were the hypocrites (المنافقون) and those in whose hearts is a sickness from the people of Medina who did not go out with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). However, the context of the verse, which clearly indicates their presence and their saying this at the meeting of the two hosts, rejects this.

In the narration of Abu Hurayrah — as reported in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Tabarani in *al-Awsat* from him — the wording is: "Utbah ibn Rabi'ah and people with him from the polytheists said on the day of Badr: 'Their religion has deluded these people.' Then Allah revealed: **إِذْ يَقُولُ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ غَرَّ هؤُلاءِ دِينُهُمْ** 'When the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness said: Their religion has deluded these people.'" What he mentioned does not apply to the verse at all, for the Noble Quran does not call the polytheists (المشركين) 'hypocrites' (منافقين) nor 'those in whose hearts is a sickness' (الذين في قلوبهم مرض).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu Ali al-Mahmudi from his father in a marfu' (رفعه) — *a narration traced back to an Imam* — report regarding the saying of Allah: **يَضْرِبُونَ وُجُوهَهُمْ وَأَدْبارَهُمْ** "striking their faces and their backs" (8:50), he said: "He only intended their posteriors (أستاههم). Indeed, Allah is Generous and uses metaphors (يكني)."

In *Tafsir al-Safi*, from *al-Kafi* from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "Indeed, Allah sent a prophet from His prophets to his people and revealed to him: 'Say to your people: There is no people of a town nor any group of people who were in obedience to Me — and I granted them prosperity therein — then they turned from what I love to what I dislike, except that I turned from what they love to what they dislike. And there is no people of a town nor any household who were in disobedience to Me — and they were afflicted with adversity therein — then they turned from what I dislike to what I love, except that I turned from what they dislike to what they love.'"

In the same source, also from him (عليه السلام), he said: "My father used to say: 'Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has decreed a final decree (قضاء حتما) that He does not bestow a blessing upon a servant and then strip him of it until the servant commits a sin by which he deserves vengeance (النقمة).'"
